title: Postpositions
description: Hindi's 'prepositions' come AFTER the noun — learn mein, par, ko, se, and ka/ki/ke.
culturalNote: Unlike English prepositions (before the noun), Hindi postpositions come after. 'Ghar mein' = in the house (literally: house in). Once this clicks, sentences start making sense. These 5 postpositions cover 90% of everyday usage in Delhi conversations.

exercises.note: 'Ghar mein' = in the house. 'Delhi mein' = in Delhi. 'Bag mein' = in the bag. 'Dil mein' = in the heart. Comes AFTER the noun. 'Kya tum ghar mein ho?' = are you inside the house? The most common postposition.

'Table par' = on the table. 'Ghar par' = at home. 'Phone par' = on the phone. 'Woh abhi ghar par hai' = she's at home right now. 'Station par milte hain' = let's meet at the station. Par = surface or location.

'Usse ko' → 'usse' = to him/her. 'Mujhe' = to me. 'Ko' marks the recipient. 'Mujhe bhookh lagi hai' = I'm hungry (hunger is happening TO me). 'Usse bolo' = tell him/her. Often fused: 'mujhe', 'tujhe', 'usse'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'The keys are on the table'?

exercises.note: Multi-purpose: 'Delhi se' = from Delhi. 'Bus se' = by bus. 'Usse' = with him/from him. 'Dil se' = from the heart (sincerely). 'Kab se?' = since when? 'Se' marks origin, means of transport, or comparison: 'mujhse better' = better than me.

Matches the OWNED thing's gender: 'Rohan ka ghar' (house=m) = Rohan's house. 'Rohan ki car' (car=f) = Rohan's car. 'Rohan ke kapde' (clothes=pl) = Rohan's clothes. This is the hardest postposition — the owner doesn't change, only the owned thing's gender affects ka/ki/ke.

exercises.sentence: Main kal Mumbai _____ aayi. Bahut thak gayi.
exercises.sentenceEnglish: I came from Mumbai yesterday. Got very tired.

exercises.note: 'Ke saath' = with (a person). 'Tum kiske saath aayi?' = who did you come with? 'Main apni dost ke saath gayi' = I went with my friend. 'Mere saath' = with me. Note: 'ke saath' for people, 'se' for instruments/means.

exercises.prompt: Your bag is inside the car. How do you say this?

exercises.note: 'Khaane ke baad' = after eating. 'Kaam ke baad milte hain' = let's meet after work. 'Baad mein' = later. Very common in time expressions. 'Is ke baad' = after this. 'Ghar jaane ke baad' = after going home.
'Khaane ke pehle' = before eating. 'Jaane ke pehle mujhe bata' = tell me before you go. 'Pehle' alone = first/before (as adverb). 'Pehle aao, phir baat karte hain' = come first, then we'll talk.

exercises.instruction: Match the postposition to its meaning

exercises.sentence: Main apni saheli _____ saath mall gayi.
exercises.sentenceEnglish: I went to the mall with my friend.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'This is Priya's phone'? (Phone is masculine)
